content.text: Incident Type Taxonomy for AI Service Failures

Agentic AI systems fail in ways that differ fundamentally from conventional software. A web server either returns a response or it does not; an LLM-backed agent can return a response that is confidently wrong, subtly harmful, or legally problematic while all infrastructure metrics stay green. Effective incident management therefore requires a taxonomy that distinguishes capability failures from infrastructure failures from safety failures—so the right team is paged, the right runbook is opened, and the right remediation is applied.
The Five Root Categories
All AI service incidents map to one of five root categories, each with distinct ownership, detection signals, and response patterns.

attrs.alt: Incident type taxonomy tree for agentic AI systems

content.text: Category 1: Capability Failures

Capability failures occur when the model produces output that is structurally valid but semantically wrong relative to the task. These incidents are invisible to standard uptime monitors because the service is technically responsive.
Hallucination: the model asserts facts that are false, fabricates citations, or invents tool outputs. Detection requires output-quality eval metrics, not latency metrics.

Tool-call failure cascade: a tool returns an error; the agent retries in a loop, expending token budget and possibly taking unintended compensating actions. Detected via loop-counter spans exceeding threshold.
Context window overflow: long multi-turn conversations exceed the model context limit; the agent silently loses early-turn instructions, producing inconsistent behavior.

Reasoning loop: the agent cycles through the same plan-act-observe steps without making progress, burning compute budget with no terminal state. Detected via step-count span attribute exceeding max_steps.
Category 2: Infrastructure Failures

Infrastructure failures are the AI-specific analog of traditional availability incidents. They are more varied than in stateless services because of GPU memory management, token quotas, and cold-start costs for large model weights.
Rate limit exhaustion: the service hits its per-minute or per-day token quota at the upstream LLM provider, causing request failures or degraded fallback behavior.
Out-of-memory (OOM) crash: the inference process exhausts GPU VRAM, causing the container to restart; active requests are lost with no response to the caller.
Cold-start latency spike: an autoscaler brings up a new inference replica, but model weight loading takes 30-90 seconds, causing p99 latency to spike before the instance warms up.
Upstream API timeout: calls to an external LLM endpoint time out due to provider-side congestion; the agent cannot complete its plan and returns a degraded response or error.
Category 3: Safety and Alignment Failures

Safety failures occur when the agent produces output that violates content policy, legal requirements, or the operator's stated behavioral constraints. These incidents may cause no technical errors yet carry the highest reputational and regulatory risk.
Policy violation: the agent's output contains prohibited content that bypassed the safety classifier.
Harmful action: the agent executes a real-world action—sending an email, cancelling an order, modifying a database record—that is harmful or contrary to user intent despite a technically valid plan.
Instruction override (jailbreak): an adversarial user prompt causes the model to ignore its system prompt constraints, producing out-of-scope or harmful responses.
Bias amplification: the agent systematically produces outputs that discriminate against a protected class in ways hard to detect in individual responses but visible in aggregate analysis.
Category 4: Integration Failures
Agentic systems interact with external tools, APIs, and databases at runtime. Integration failures occur when those external dependencies behave unexpectedly.

Authentication failure: the agent's tool calls are rejected because a secret rotated, an OAuth token expired, or an API key was revoked. The agent may retry with the same invalid credentials, amplifying the failure.
Schema drift: a downstream API changes its response format; the agent's tool-result parser throws an exception or silently returns empty data, causing downstream reasoning errors.
Data staleness: the agent retrieves stale context from a vector store or cache and acts on outdated information, producing incorrect but confident recommendations.
Category 5: Security Incidents
Prompt injection: malicious content in a retrieved document or user turn hijacks the agent's tool-call sequence to exfiltrate data or execute unauthorized actions.
Credential leakage: API keys, passwords, or PII appear in trace spans, logs, or model outputs that are accessible to unintended parties.
Unauthorized data access: a multi-tenant agent accesses data belonging to a different tenant due to a missing or bypassed authorization check on a tool call.
Taxonomy YAML Configuration
Codify the taxonomy in a machine-readable config so alert routing rules, runbook links, and owner mappings can be maintained in one place and consumed by your incident tooling.

# incident-taxonomy.yaml
taxonomy:
  version: "1.0"
  categories:
    - id: capability
      label: "Capability Failure"
      owner_team: ai-platform
      default_severity: SEV-3
      escalation_severity: SEV-1
      types:
        - id: hallucination
          label: "Model Hallucination"
          detection_signal: "output_quality_eval_score < 0.6"
          runbook: "runbooks/capability/hallucination.md"
        - id: tool_call_loop
          label: "Tool Call Retry Loop"
          detection_signal: "agent.step_count > max_steps"
          runbook: "runbooks/capability/tool-loop.md"
        - id: context_overflow
          label: "Context Window Overflow"
          detection_signal: "token_usage_ratio > 0.95"
          runbook: "runbooks/capability/context-overflow.md"
        - id: reasoning_loop
          label: "Reasoning Loop / No Progress"
          detection_signal: "repeated_action_hash_count > 3"
          runbook: "runbooks/capability/reasoning-loop.md"

    - id: infrastructure
      label: "Infrastructure Failure"
      owner_team: sre
      default_severity: SEV-2
      escalation_severity: SEV-1
      types:
        - id: rate_limit
          label: "LLM Rate Limit Exhaustion"
          detection_signal: "http_429_rate > 0.05"
          runbook: "runbooks/infra/rate-limit.md"
        - id: oom
          label: "Out of Memory / GPU Crash"
          detection_signal: "container_oom_kill_total > 0"
          runbook: "runbooks/infra/oom.md"
        - id: cold_start_latency
          label: "Inference Cold-Start Spike"
          detection_signal: "p99_latency_ms > 5000 AND new_replica_started"
          runbook: "runbooks/infra/cold-start.md"

    - id: safety
      label: "Safety / Alignment Failure"
      owner_team: trust-and-safety
      default_severity: SEV-2
      escalation_severity: SEV-1
      notify_legal: true
      types:
        - id: policy_violation
          label: "Content Policy Violation"
          detection_signal: "safety_classifier_block_rate > 0.001"
          runbook: "runbooks/safety/policy-violation.md"
        - id: harmful_action
          label: "Harmful Real-World Action"
          detection_signal: "user_harm_report OR automated_action_reversal"
          runbook: "runbooks/safety/harmful-action.md"
        - id: jailbreak
          label: "Instruction Override / Jailbreak"
          detection_signal: "system_prompt_adherence_score < 0.5"
          runbook: "runbooks/safety/jailbreak.md"

    - id: integration
      label: "Integration Failure"
      owner_team: platform-integrations
      default_severity: SEV-3
      escalation_severity: SEV-2
      types:
        - id: auth_failure
          label: "Tool Authentication Failure"
          detection_signal: "tool_call_http_401_rate > 0.02"
          runbook: "runbooks/integration/auth-failure.md"
        - id: schema_drift
          label: "API Schema Drift"
          detection_signal: "tool_result_parse_error_rate > 0.01"
          runbook: "runbooks/integration/schema-drift.md"

    - id: security
      label: "Security Incident"
      owner_team: security
      default_severity: SEV-1
      escalation_severity: SEV-1
      notify_ciso: true
      types:
        - id: prompt_injection
          label: "Prompt Injection Attack"
          detection_signal: "injection_pattern_match OR anomalous_tool_sequence"
          runbook: "runbooks/security/prompt-injection.md"
        - id: credential_leak
          label: "Credential / PII Leakage"
          detection_signal: "pii_in_trace OR secret_pattern_in_output"
          runbook: "runbooks/security/credential-leak.md"
Python Classification Helper

Load the taxonomy config and expose a classify_incident function that maps runtime signals to a (category, type, severity) triple. This function is called by your alerting webhook handler to enrich incoming PagerDuty or OpsGenie payloads before routing.
Alert Routing by Incident Type
Wire the taxonomy to your alert manager so that each incident type routes to the correct on-call rotation with the correct runbook pre-attached.
Category
Example Type
On-Call Team
Response SLA
Auto-Actions
Capability
Hallucination, loop
AI Platform
15 min acknowledge
Flag session for review
Infrastructure
Rate limit, OOM
SRE
5 min acknowledge
Scale-out trigger
Safety
Policy violation
Trust and Safety
Immediate page
Suspend endpoint
Integration
Auth failure
Platform Integrations
30 min acknowledge
Fallback route
Security
Prompt injection
Security and SRE
Immediate page
Kill switch and audit
Prometheus Alert Rules for Each Category
The following PrometheusRule covers one alert per taxonomy category. Each alert carries type and category labels that drive alert manager routing to the correct on-call rotation.

# prometheus-ai-alerts.yaml
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: PrometheusRule
metadata:
  name: ai-incident-taxonomy-alerts
  namespace: monitoring
spec:
  groups:
    - name: capability_failures
      interval: 30s
      rules:
        - alert: AgentReasoningLoop
          expr: |
            max by (agent_id, session_id) (
              agent_step_count{job="agentic-service"}
            ) > 20
          for: 1m
          labels:
            severity: warning
            incident_category: capability
            incident_type: reasoning_loop
            team: ai-platform
          annotations:
            summary: "Agent {{ $labels.agent_id }} stuck in reasoning loop"
            description: "Step count {{ $value }} exceeds max_steps threshold."
            runbook: "https://wiki.internal/runbooks/capability/reasoning-loop"

        - alert: OutputQualityDegradation
          expr: |
            avg_over_time(
              output_quality_eval_score{job="agentic-service"}[5m]
            ) < 0.6
          for: 5m
          labels:
            severity: warning
            incident_category: capability
            incident_type: hallucination
            team: ai-platform
          annotations:
            summary: "Output quality score below threshold"
            description: "5m avg eval score: {{ $value }}"
            runbook: "https://wiki.internal/runbooks/capability/hallucination"

    - name: infrastructure_failures
      interval: 30s
      rules:
        - alert: LLMRateLimitExhaustion
          expr: |
            rate(llm_http_responses_total{
              status_code="429",job="agentic-service"
            }[5m]) > 0.05
          for: 2m
          labels:
            severity: critical
            incident_category: infrastructure
            incident_type: rate_limit
            team: sre
          annotations:
            summary: "LLM provider rate limit hit"
            runbook: "https://wiki.internal/runbooks/infra/rate-limit"

    - name: safety_failures
      interval: 15s
      rules:
        - alert: SafetyPolicyViolationSpike
          expr: |
            rate(safety_classifier_blocks_total{job="agentic-service"}[5m])
            / rate(llm_requests_total{job="agentic-service"}[5m]) > 0.001
          for: 1m
          labels:
            severity: critical
            incident_category: safety
            incident_type: policy_violation
            team: trust-and-safety
          annotations:
            summary: "Safety classifier block rate elevated"
            runbook: "https://wiki.internal/runbooks/safety/policy-violation"

    - name: security_incidents
      interval: 15s
      rules:
        - alert: PotentialPromptInjection
          expr: |
            rate(tool_calls_flagged_injection_total{job="agentic-service"}[5m]) > 0
          for: 0m
          labels:
            severity: critical
            incident_category: security
            incident_type: prompt_injection
            team: security
          annotations:
            summary: "Possible prompt injection detected"
            runbook: "https://wiki.internal/runbooks/security/prompt-injection"
Taxonomy Versioning and Governance
A taxonomy is only useful when every team uses the same version. Establish a lightweight governance process:
Store incident-taxonomy.yaml in a shared internal repository under a CODEOWNERS rule that requires approval from AI Platform, SRE, Trust and Safety, and Security before merging changes.
Version the file with a semver field. Alert routing and runbook tooling reads the version at startup and logs a warning if it is more than one minor version behind the published latest.
Review the taxonomy quarterly in the major incident review forum. New incident types discovered in postmortems that do not fit existing categories are strong signals to add a new type or category.
Each incident type must have a runbook path before it can be merged. Automated CI checks that the referenced runbook file exists in the runbooks repository.
Dual-Category Incidents

Many real incidents span two categories simultaneously. A single event can be both a capability failure and a security incident. A prompt injection attack that causes a reasoning loop produces signals from both categories at once.

Resolution rule: when an incident fires multiple taxonomy alerts simultaneously, the highest-severity category wins for routing. The incident record must note all matched categories so postmortems address root causes across all of them. A prompt injection that causes a reasoning loop is a security incident whose consequence was a capability failure, not simply a capability failure.
Instrumenting for Taxonomy Coverage

Each category requires specific instrumentation to generate the signals its detection rules depend on. Teams that skip instrumentation for a category are blind to incidents in that category until a user reports them.
Capability coverage: instrument agent step count, output eval score, token usage ratio, and action hash deduplication in each AgentExecutor span.
Infrastructure coverage: track LLM HTTP response codes (429, 503), GPU memory utilization, inference replica count, and p99 first-token latency per model version.
Safety coverage: publish a safety_classifier_blocks_total counter with block_reason labels; instrument system_prompt_adherence_score as a histogram if you have a model-graded adherence check.
Integration coverage: record HTTP status codes per tool name as tool_call_http_status_total with tool and status_code labels; track parse errors as tool_result_parse_errors_total.

Security coverage: run an injection pattern scanner on all retrieved external content before it enters the model context; emit tool_calls_flagged_injection_total when a pattern matches. Log all PII-in-output detections from your redaction pipeline.
Key Takeaways
The five categories—capability, infrastructure, safety, integration, security—map to distinct owner teams, detection signals, and remediation actions. Conflating them delays response.
Codify the taxonomy in YAML so alert routing rules and runbook links are centrally maintained and version-controlled. Every alert should carry incident_category and incident_type labels.
Safety and security incidents auto-escalate to SEV-1 regardless of apparent user impact—the reputational and legal risk justifies the higher response cost.
When multiple categories fire simultaneously, route by highest severity and record all matched categories in the incident document to ensure complete postmortem coverage.
Review and extend the taxonomy quarterly. New incident types discovered in postmortems indicate coverage gaps in detection that the taxonomy must evolve to capture.
